questions.prompt: What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

choices.text: An open standard for exposing tools, resources, and prompt templates to LLMs uniformly. Build an integration once as an MCP server; any MCP-aware client (Claude Desktop, IDE, your own app) can use it. Replaces one-off integrations duplicated across apps.

questions.explanation: MCP is the deployment-and-reuse layer that sits on top of tool calling. It standardizes how tools, resources (read-only data), and prompts are exposed so the same integration works across multiple LLM-powered apps without rewriting.

questions.prompt: You're considering writing an MCP server for a tool. When is MCP NOT the right choice?

choices.text: When the tool is one-off and specific to a single app — keep it as an inline tool in that app's Messages API call. Promote to MCP only when you find yourself copying the integration into a second app or want to use it from Claude Desktop.
When the tool will be reused across apps.
When you want Claude Desktop access.

questions.explanation: Inline tools (defined in the API call) are simpler when scope is single-app. MCP servers add value when reuse, sharing, or Claude Desktop access is in scope. Start inline; promote to MCP when the value of standalone-service architecture exceeds the cost of running a separate process.
